name: Chad
tagline: From Toumaï and the Sahel empires to the heart of Africa
summary: Chad straddles the Sahara, the Sahel and the wooded south, with Lake Chad as its ancient pivot. Its basin yielded Toumaï, a seven-million-year-old hominin, and nurtured the Sao culture and the great trans-Saharan empires of Kanem and Kanem-Bornu alongside the eastern sultanates of Baguirmi and Wadai. Conquered piecemeal by France, it became independent in 1960 and endured decades of civil war, conflict with Libya, and authoritarian rule under Hissène Habré and the Déby dynasty.

founding.label: Independence from France
founding.detail: The Republic of Chad attained full independence from France on 11 August 1960, with the southern trade-union leader François (later Ngarta) Tombalbaye as its first president.

quickFacts.label: Defining feature
quickFacts.value: Lake Chad basin, where the Sahara meets the Sahel

quickFacts.label: Oldest claim to fame
quickFacts.value: Toumaï (Sahelanthropus tchadensis), c. 7 million years old

eras.title: Toumaï and the Sao
eras.standfirst: A hominin skull in the Djurab desert and a lost lake-basin culture set Chad's deepest record.
body: In 2001 a French-led team working in the Djurab desert of northern Chad uncovered a remarkably complete cranium, nicknamed Toumaï, belonging to a new species named Sahelanthropus tchadensis. Dated to roughly seven million years, it is among the oldest known members of the human lineage, suggesting that key chapters of human evolution unfolded far from the East African Rift, in what is now one of the planet's harshest deserts.

Far later, from around the sixth century BCE, the Sao civilisation flourished along the lower Chari River and the southern shores of Lake Chad, across present-day Chad, Cameroon and Nigeria. The Sao left no writing but are known through hundreds of archaeological sites and their distinctive terracotta and bronze figures of humans and animals, together with funerary urns, jewellery and decorated pottery.

The Sao endured into the medieval period before being absorbed by the rising states of the Lake Chad basin; peoples such as the Kotoko, Buduma and Kanembu trace descent from them. Their fortified mound settlements mark the basin as a long-inhabited centre of population, trade and craft, the seedbed from which Chad's later empires grew.

events.title: Discovery of Toumaï
events.summary: A team led by Michel Brunet recovered the cranium of Sahelanthropus tchadensis at the Toros-Menalla (TM 266) site in the Djurab desert; the species was announced in 2002 and dated to about seven million years.

events.title: Rise of the Sao culture
events.summary: The Sao settled the lower Chari and southern Lake Chad, producing celebrated terracotta and bronze figures across more than 350 known sites before being absorbed by later states.

eras.title: Kanem and Kanem-Bornu
eras.standfirst: A thousand-year empire on the trans-Saharan trade routes, anchored by Lake Chad and the Sayfawa dynasty.
body: From about the eighth century the empire of Kanem rose on the north-eastern side of Lake Chad, controlling the southern end of the caravan road to Tripoli. Its wealth came from the trans-Saharan trade: horses, salt and manufactured goods moved south, while enslaved people captured in raids to the south, together with ivory and other goods, moved north. In the eleventh century the ruling Duguwa gave way to the Sayfawa dynasty, traditionally founded by Mai Hummay, whose conversion made Islam the religion of the court.

Kanem reached its zenith in the thirteenth century under Mai Dunama Dibalemi (reigned c. 1210–1248), who commanded a large cavalry and extended influence from the Fezzan in modern Libya to the savanna south of the lake. Internal strife and pressure from the Bulala forced the Sayfawa to abandon their capital Njimi around the 1380s and relocate west of Lake Chad to Bornu, where they refounded the state and built a new capital at Ngazargamu.

The reconstituted empire of Kanem-Bornu peaked again in the late sixteenth century under Mai Idris Alooma, who imported firearms and Ottoman military advisers and pushed Islamic law. From the early nineteenth century the empire was shaken by the Fulani jihad and was effectively rescued and then ruled by the scholar-soldier Muhammad al-Kanemi; the ancient line of mais was formally ended in 1846, and the al-Kanemi shehus governed the rump of Bornu thereafter.

events.title: Hummay founds the Sayfawa dynasty
events.summary: Mai Hummay, the first Muslim ruler of Kanem, displaced the Duguwa and founded the Sayfawa (Sefuwa) dynasty, which would rule the empire for some seven centuries.

events.title: Zenith under Dunama Dibalemi
events.summary: Mai Dunama Dibalemi brought Kanem to its peak, projecting power from the Fezzan to the southern savanna and deepening trade and diplomatic ties with North Africa and Egypt.

events.title: Sayfawa flee Kanem for Bornu
events.summary: Pressed by the Bulala, the Sayfawa abandoned Njimi and re-established the state in Bornu, west of Lake Chad, later founding the capital Ngazargamu.

events.title: Golden age under Idris Alooma
events.summary: Mai Idris Alooma modernised the army with Ottoman firearms and advisers and promoted Islamic law, leading Kanem-Bornu to its second peak.

events.title: End of the Sayfawa line
events.summary: The traditional office of mai was abolished in 1846 after the al-Kanemi shehus, who had risen during the Fulani jihad, took full control of Bornu.

figures.name: Idris Alooma
figures.blurb: Reforming warrior-king who reorganised the army with imported firearms and Ottoman military advisers and enforced Islamic law, presiding over Kanem-Bornu's late-16th-century golden age.

eras.title: Baguirmi, Wadai and the Saharan trade
eras.standfirst: East of Lake Chad, rival Muslim sultanates grew rich on caravans of salt, kola and captives.
body: Beyond Bornu, two Islamic sultanates dominated the region between Lake Chad and Darfur. Baguirmi, south-east of the lake, emerged in the sixteenth century and adopted Islam, while Wadai (Ouaddaï) arose to its north-east. Both were intermittently tributary to Bornu yet developed their own courts, armies and commerce, fighting and trading with one another and with their larger neighbour.

Wadai was founded around 1635 when Abd al-Karim led a Muslim revolt that overthrew the ruling Tunjur and established a new sultanate centred eventually on Abéché. From the early nineteenth century, under rulers such as Muhammad Sabun, Wadai expanded and grew wealthy by commanding caravan routes that linked Abéché northward to Benghazi on the Mediterranean and eastward toward Darfur and the Nile.

These states were deeply tied to the trans-Saharan economy. Salt, natron, kola nuts, ivory, cloth and especially enslaved people moved along their routes; Baguirmi and Wadai both raided southern peoples for captives, and Wadai is often described as the last of the great Sudanic empires to control the Saharan slave trade. That commerce shaped the social order of the Chadian basin until European conquest dismantled it.

events.title: Founding of the Wadai Sultanate
events.summary: Abd al-Karim led an Islamic revolt that overthrew the Tunjur and established the Wadai (Ouaddaï) sultanate east of Lake Chad, ruled from Abéché.

events.title: Wadai's commercial expansion
events.summary: Under Muhammad Sabun, Wadai expanded and prospered by controlling caravan routes from Abéché north to Benghazi and the east-west trade between Darfur and Bornu.

events.title: Baguirmi and the slave trade
events.summary: The sultanate of Baguirmi, Islamic from the sixteenth century, supplied large numbers of captives to the trans-Saharan slave trade alongside skins, ivory and cotton.

eras.title: Rabih's empire and French conquest
eras.standfirst: A Sudanese warlord overran the basin, then fell to France, which folded Chad into Equatorial Africa.
body: In the late nineteenth century the warlord Rabih az-Zubayr, leading a well-armed and disciplined private army out of the Sudan, swept across the region. Between 1892 and 1894 he conquered Bornu and made himself master of much of the Lake Chad basin, ruling it as a militarised state and clashing with Baguirmi and other powers just as France was advancing toward the lake from three directions.

French columns converging from Congo, Niger and Algeria met near the Chari River, and on 22 April 1900 they defeated and killed Rabih at the Battle of Kousséri; the French commander, Major Amédée-François Lamy, also died in the fighting. The victory opened the basin to French control. France subdued the sultanates over the following years and constituted Chad as a colonial territory, attaching it to French Equatorial Africa in 1910.

Colonial rule was thin and uneven: the cotton-growing south was developed and administered far more closely than the vast Muslim north and the Saharan Borkou-Ennedi-Tibesti, a neglect that hardened regional divisions. In 1946 Chad became an overseas territory of the French Republic; an autonomous republic within the French Community was proclaimed in 1958, leading toward full sovereignty.

events.title: Rabih conquers Bornu
events.summary: Rabih az-Zubayr overran the Kanem-Bornu lands with one of the best-equipped armies in sub-Saharan Africa, ruling the basin as a military dictatorship.

events.title: Battle of Kousséri
events.summary: Converging French columns defeated and killed Rabih near the Chari River; the French commander Major Lamy also fell, ending Rabih's empire and opening the basin to France.

events.title: Chad joins French Equatorial Africa
events.summary: France organised the conquered territory and incorporated Chad into the federation of French Equatorial Africa, governing the south far more closely than the Muslim north.

events.title: Autonomous republic proclaimed
events.summary: Chad became an autonomous republic within the French Community, a step toward the full independence achieved in 1960.

figures.name: Rabih az-Zubayr
figures.role: Sudanese warlord and conqueror of Bornu
figures.blurb: Built a formidable private army and conquered the Lake Chad basin in the 1890s before being defeated and killed by the French at Kousséri in 1900.

eras.title: Independence and civil war
eras.standfirst: Sovereignty in 1960 gave way to north–south insurgency, coups, Libyan intervention and the rise of Habré.
body: Chad became independent on 11 August 1960 under François (later Ngarta) Tombalbaye, a southerner whose increasingly authoritarian, southern-dominated rule alienated the largely Muslim north and centre. In 1966 the rebel front FROLINAT took up arms, beginning a civil war that would define the country. Tombalbaye was killed in a military coup in 1975, which brought General Félix Malloum to power without ending the fighting.

Through the late 1970s FROLINAT's factions, several backed by Libya, contested the country; rebels reached the capital and in 1979 Goukouni Oueddei and Hissène Habré joined a fragile transitional government that soon collapsed into renewed war between them. Libya, which occupied the Aouzou Strip in the north, intervened repeatedly on Goukouni's side. In 1982 Habré seized N'Djamena and the presidency.

Habré consolidated a harsh one-party state while fighting Libya. In 1987, in the so-called Toyota War, his mobile forces inflicted heavy defeats on the Libyan army and drove it back toward the Aouzou Strip. His regime, however, became notorious for political killings and torture: a later truth commission and court would attribute tens of thousands of deaths to it. In December 1990 Habré was overthrown by his former army commander, Idriss Déby.

events.title: Independence under Tombalbaye
events.summary: Chad gained full independence from France, with François Tombalbaye as first president; his southern-centred, authoritarian rule soon stoked northern grievances.

events.title: FROLINAT and the civil war
events.summary: The National Liberation Front of Chad (FROLINAT) launched an insurgency rooted in northern and Muslim discontent, beginning decades of civil war.

events.title: Coup kills Tombalbaye
events.summary: Tombalbaye was assassinated in a military coup; General Félix Malloum took power but could not end the conflict with FROLINAT's factions.

events.title: Habré seizes power
events.summary: Hissène Habré captured N'Djamena and made himself president, establishing a repressive regime amid continuing war and Libyan intervention.

events.title: The Toyota War with Libya
events.summary: Chadian forces under Hassan Djamous won a string of victories at Fada, Bir Kora and Ouadi Doum, driving the Libyan army back toward the disputed Aouzou Strip.

figures.name: François (Ngarta) Tombalbaye
figures.blurb: Southern trade-union leader who led Chad to independence in 1960; his authoritarian, southern-dominated rule provoked the northern insurgency, and he was killed in the 1975 coup.

eras.title: The Déby era and its legacy
eras.standfirst: Three decades of Déby rule, an oil economy, a landmark war-crimes verdict, and a dynastic succession.
body: Idriss Déby took power in December 1990 and ruled Chad for thirty years. He introduced a multiparty constitution in 1996 and won repeated elections widely criticised as flawed, while surviving rebellions that twice reached the capital. From the early 2000s an oil pipeline gave the state new revenues, and Déby positioned Chad's army as a key Western partner against jihadist groups in the Sahel and around Lake Chad.

The crimes of the earlier Habré regime were eventually answered in court. In 2016 the Extraordinary African Chambers in Senegal convicted Hissène Habré of crimes against humanity, war crimes and torture, sentencing him to life imprisonment; the verdict, upheld on appeal in 2017, was the first time a former head of state was convicted of such crimes in another country's courts under universal jurisdiction. The earlier Aouzou dispute had already been settled when the International Court of Justice awarded the strip to Chad in 1994.

Idriss Déby was killed in April 2021 while visiting troops fighting a rebel incursion, days after claiming a sixth term. A military council immediately installed his son, General Mahamat Idriss Déby, as transitional head of state, a step many regarded as an unconstitutional dynastic succession. Mahamat Déby consolidated power and was declared winner of a presidential election in 2024, extending the family's hold on Chad.

events.title: Idriss Déby seizes power
events.summary: Leading the Patriotic Salvation Movement (MPS) from Sudan, Déby overthrew Habré and installed himself as president, beginning a thirty-year rule.

events.title: ICJ awards Aouzou Strip to Chad
events.summary: The International Court of Justice ruled that the disputed Aouzou Strip belonged to Chad; Libyan forces withdrew by the end of May 1994.

events.title: Habré convicted of crimes against humanity
events.summary: The Extraordinary African Chambers in Senegal sentenced Hissène Habré to life for crimes against humanity, war crimes and torture, a landmark of universal jurisdiction; the verdict was upheld on appeal in 2017.

events.title: Death of Idriss Déby; son takes power
events.summary: Déby died of wounds from fighting on the front line; a military council named his son, General Mahamat Idriss Déby, transitional president, widely seen as an unconstitutional succession.

figures.name: Idriss Déby Itno
figures.blurb: Seized power in 1990 and ruled for three decades as a key Western military ally in the Sahel; killed in 2021 during a rebel offensive, after which his son succeeded him.

figures.name: Hissène Habré
figures.blurb: Wartime leader who defeated Libya in 1987 but ran a regime blamed for tens of thousands of deaths; convicted of crimes against humanity in Senegal in 2016.

pullquote.text: The first time the domestic courts of one country tried and convicted the former ruler of another for serious international crimes under universal jurisdiction.
pullquote.attribution: Human Rights Watch, on the 2016 Habré verdict

figures.name: Idris Alooma
figures.blurb: Brought Kanem-Bornu to its late-16th-century peak through military reform with Ottoman firearms and the promotion of Islamic law.

figures.name: Rabih az-Zubayr
figures.role: Conqueror of the Lake Chad basin
figures.blurb: Sudanese warlord who overran Bornu in the 1890s and was defeated and killed by France at Kousséri in 1900.

figures.name: Hissène Habré
figures.blurb: Defeated Libya in the 1987 Toyota War but led a brutal regime; convicted of crimes against humanity in Senegal in 2016.
